sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Alcohol withdrawal ranges from tremor and anxiety to seizures, delirium, hyperthermia, and cardiovascular collapse. Prehospital priorities are identifying severe withdrawal, excluding other medical causes of agitation or reduced conscious state, treating seizures, maintaining staff safety, and transporting high-risk patients to hospital.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Recent reduction or cessation of heavy alcohol use, often after prolonged heavy drinking
- Mild withdrawal within hours to the first day: tremor, anxiety, insomnia, diaphoresis, nausea, or vomiting
- Progression over 24 to 48 hours: marked tremor, tachycardia, hypertension, pyrexia, or autonomic overactivity
- Severe withdrawal after 48 hours or more: agitation, hallucinations, confusion, or delirium tremens
- Brief generalised seizures, often early in withdrawal

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Agitation or confusion may be caused by hypoglycaemia, head injury, infection, polysubstance use, hepatic encephalopathy, or a primary psychiatric disorder rather than alcohol withdrawal alone
- Severe withdrawal and delirium tremens are medical emergencies with significant mortality risk
- Persistent anxiety alone is not the same as severe withdrawal; prolonged diazepam use for ongoing anxiety can be harmful
- A history of previous severe withdrawal, seizures, delirium, early-morning drinking to relieve symptoms, or major comorbidity increases risk

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Check BGL, temperature, conscious state, and full vital signs early
- Give oxygen only if clinically indicated
- Keep the environment calm, reduce unnecessary stimulation, and maintain staff safety
- Manage seizures under the seizure guideline if they occur
- Prioritise transport for hallucinations, severe agitation, hyperthermia, seizure, persistent vomiting, or suspected delirium tremens

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG and cardiac monitoring where severe withdrawal, chest pain, or significant autonomic disturbance is present
- Gain IV access where severe withdrawal, dehydration, recurrent vomiting, or seizure risk is present
- Treat hypoglycaemia, hyperthermia, or dehydration within local protocol
- Consider protocol-led benzodiazepine therapy only for severe withdrawal, seizures, or dangerous agitation and only within scope or senior direction
- Escalate early for delirium, persistent abnormal vital signs, recurrent seizure, or concern for mixed pathology

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Document last drink if known, previous withdrawal severity, seizure history, co-ingested substances, and any current medical illness
- A post-ictal patient who is calm and drowsy should still prompt consideration of other seizure causes rather than reflexively attributing everything to alcohol withdrawal
- A patient may still be at risk of alcohol withdrawal despite appearing intoxicated
